prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
I think the idea of open-plan offices is often overrated. While these offices are designed to encourage collaboration and communication between coworkers, they can also lead to a significant increase in distractions and a decrease in personal space, which can actually decrease productivity and increase stress levels. In addition, open plan offices may require people to work in close proximity to their colleagues who have different time schedules and work on different tasks, which can further reduce the benefits of the collaborative workspace. Moreover, in the era of Covid-19, open plan offices present additional health challenges related to the potential spread of the virus. Therefore, while open plan offices have been widely adopted and praised in recent years, I believe their benefits have been overstated, and more companies should consider alternatives, such as assigning separate rooms or workspaces to each individual or small team.

In my view, a much better alternative to the open plan office is the concept of quiet and separate spaces for deep work, which is the time when an individual can work on a task, with few or no interruptions and with the full focus and attention that is required by the most important challenges in our work today. This idea, recently popularized by the book "Deep Work: Rules for Focused Successful Execute," by Cal Newport, proposes that individuals or small teams have separate high-quality workspaces, which are designed to support highly focused work on a specific project for a certain period of time each day. I believe that this idea is set to become significantly more popular in the years to come, particularly given the current global challenges.
